maya-lindqvist.name: Maya Lindqvist
maya-lindqvist.bio: Maya has spent the last four years wiring large language models into content management systems, and the ten years before that regretting SQL queries. She maintains two contrib modules in the Drupal AI ecosystem and speaks regularly about retrieval-augmented generation. She lives in Gothenburg with an elderly greyhound named Bertrand.

tomas-okafor.name: Tomás Okafor
tomas-okafor.bio: Tomás keeps several hundred production clusters boring, which is exactly how he likes them. A recovering perfectionist, he now teaches teams to ship smaller and roll back faster. He once deleted a production database and lived to write the postmortem, which he considers his finest publication.

priya-raman.name: Priya Raman
priya-raman.bio: Priya audits websites for accessibility and then, more importantly, helps the teams who built them understand what they were missing. She is a screen reader power user, a WCAG working group contributor, and firmly of the opinion that accessible sites are simply better sites.

jonas-weber.name: Jonas Weber
jonas-weber.bio: Jonas designs hosting platforms for European agencies and has strong feelings about Kubernetes that he mostly keeps to himself until the second coffee. He co-organises the Zurich DevOps meetup and contributes to several open source deployment tools.

amara-diallo.name: Amara Diallo
amara-diallo.bio: Amara builds design systems for multilingual, multi-brand sites across West Africa and Europe. She is an advocate for shipping less JavaScript and letting the browser do what browsers are now astonishingly good at. Her CSS talks are famous for live-coding without a safety net.

erik-sorensen.name: Erik Sørensen
erik-sorensen.bio: Erik has been writing PHP since before it was cool the first time. He works on high-traffic publishing platforms and is the author of a queue-processing library used by newsrooms on three continents. He believes most scaling problems are actually queueing problems in disguise.

lucia-fernandez.name: Lucía Fernández
lucia-fernandez.bio: Lucía sits at the intersection of design and engineering, translating brand guidelines into design tokens and components that survive contact with real content editors. She has rebuilt the same design system three times and is happy to tell you what she got wrong the first two.

dmitri-petrov.name: Dmitri Petrov
dmitri-petrov.bio: Dmitri evaluates language models for a living and has developed a healthy scepticism of benchmark numbers. His current obsession is making small, cheap models do the work of large, expensive ones. He previously worked in search relevance, which he says is the same job with different marketing.

fatima-el-amrani.name: Fatima El-Amrani
fatima-el-amrani.bio: Fatima has onboarded over a thousand first-time contributors to open source projects and remembers most of their names. She runs contribution sprints at events across Europe and North Africa and believes the issue queue is a social space first and a technical one second.

henrik-visser.name: Henrik Visser
henrik-visser.bio: Henrik helps agencies move from FTP-and-prayers to proper delivery pipelines. He is an advocate of GitOps, small pull requests, and going home on time. His workshop style has been described as 'aggressively practical'.

sofia-marino.name: Sofia Marino
sofia-marino.bio: Sofia has built search features on everything from LIKE queries to distributed vector databases. She joined Vela to work on hybrid retrieval and now spends her days convincing product managers that relevance is a process, not a feature. She collects vintage synthesizers.

kwame-mensah.name: Kwame Mensah
kwame-mensah.bio: Kwame runs one of the most consistent tech meetups in West Africa, now in its seventh year. By day he builds decoupled publishing platforms; by night he wrangles venue bookings and pizza budgets. He speaks about the unglamorous logistics that keep communities alive.

ingrid-halvorsen.name: Ingrid Halvorsen
ingrid-halvorsen.bio: Ingrid looks after databases that are too big to fit in anyone's comfort zone. She has migrated petabyte-scale clusters with zero downtime and has the greying hair to prove it. Her talks feature real incident timelines, lightly anonymised to protect the guilty.

carlos-mendes.name: Carlos Mendes
carlos-mendes.bio: Carlos contributes to PHP open source tooling and has a soft spot for language internals. He reviews more code than he writes these days and considers that a promotion. He is currently documenting modern PHP idioms for teams stuck on habits from a decade ago.

aisha-khan.name: Aisha Khan
aisha-khan.bio: Aisha breaks AI systems before other people can, specialising in prompt injection and data exfiltration through language model integrations. She has responsibly disclosed vulnerabilities in a dozen popular AI products and delivers her findings with an unsettling cheerfulness.

bjorn-lund.name: Björn Lund
bjorn-lund.bio: Björn has shipped web components at scale and lived with the consequences. He cares about progressive enhancement, boring technology, and the moment when a junior developer's eyes light up because something finally clicked. He whittles spoons to cope with JavaScript framework churn.

nadia-petit.name: Nadia Petit
nadia-petit.bio: Nadia manages a distributed platform team and writes extensively about blameless culture and sustainable on-call. She was an SRE for eight years before moving into management, and she still reads every postmortem her team produces, usually with snacks.

oliver-hart.name: Oliver Hart
oliver-hart.bio: Oliver designs public APIs and webhooks that other developers have to live with, a responsibility he takes seriously. He maintains an open source API style guide and has strong, well-documented opinions about versioning. He apologises for REST-vs-GraphQL discourse he started in 2023.

greta-novak.name: Greta Novák
greta-novak.bio: Greta went from filing her first core issue to co-maintaining a subsystem in under three years, and she wrote down exactly how. She mentors new contributors through their first patches and believes the contribution ladder should have more rungs and better signage.

sam-oduya.name: Sam Oduya
sam-oduya.bio: Sam writes documentation that developers actually read, a skill acquired through years of watching them not read it. They contribute docs to several open source projects and run workshops on writing for busy, distracted, slightly grumpy audiences.

elena-vasquez.name: Elena Vásquez
elena-vasquez.bio: Elena has spent a decade building event-driven systems and has the scars to show for it. She now leads architecture reviews where her most common feedback is 'you do not need this yet'. Her cautionary tales are first-hand, expensive, and generously shared.

finn-obrien.name: Finn O'Brien
finn-obrien.bio: Finn prototypes interfaces with real content from day one, having learned the hard way that lorem ipsum hides every interesting problem. They work with cultural institutions on digital exhibitions and believe the best design tool is a conversation with an actual user.

hana-suzuki.name: Hana Suzuki
hana-suzuki.bio: Hana builds AI features that survive contact with real editorial teams. She has shipped chatbots, summarisers, and translation pipelines for publishers in Japan and Europe, and is candid about which ones users actually kept using. Her demos are rehearsed; her stories are not.

marco-ricci.name: Marco Ricci
marco-ricci.bio: Marco has led engineering at three agencies and has funded, defunded, and re-funded open source work at each of them. He talks openly about what it costs to maintain the software everyone depends on and who ends up paying. He makes a genuinely excellent carbonara.

zoe-kalindi.name: Zoë Kalindi
zoe-kalindi.bio: Zoë cut her teeth triaging an issue queue nobody else wanted and is now release manager for a widely used contrib project. She speaks about the invisible work of open source: triage, review, releases, and saying no kindly. She is powered by strong tea and stronger opinions.

sessions.title: Your First RAG Pipeline
sessions.description: Retrieval-augmented generation sounds intimidating, but at its heart it is three steps: chunk your content, index it somewhere searchable, and hand the best matches to a language model along with the user's question. In this session we build a working RAG pipeline on top of a standard CMS, live, starting from an empty index. You will see how documents become embeddings, how similarity search actually behaves with messy real-world content, and where hallucinations sneak in when retrieval goes wrong. No machine learning background needed — if you can build a website, you can follow every step.

sessions.title: Prompt Injection: Attacking Your Own Chatbot
sessions.description: Your site chatbot reads your content, and your content is user-generated. That is a security boundary, and almost nobody treats it as one. This session demonstrates live attacks against a realistic CMS-integrated assistant: indirect prompt injection via comment fields, data exfiltration through markdown image rendering, tool-call hijacking against agentic integrations, and retrieval poisoning of vector indexes. For each attack we discuss what mitigation is actually feasible versus what is security theatre. Familiarity with LLM tool-calling and RAG architectures is assumed — this is a deep end session, and we will not be recapping the basics.

sessions.title: Embeddings Are Just Coordinates
sessions.description: Vector search powers most modern AI features, yet explanations of it either drown you in linear algebra or wave their hands entirely. This talk takes the middle path. Using nothing more advanced than a map metaphor and some live visualisations, we will build an accurate intuition for what an embedding is, why 'similar meaning' becomes 'nearby points', what a vector database actually does all day, and why your search for 'affordable lodging' finds the page titled 'cheap places to stay'. You will leave able to explain semantic search to a colleague, a stakeholder, or a mildly interested parent.

sessions.title: Fine-tune or RAG? Choosing Wisely
sessions.description: Every AI feature request eventually reaches the fork in the road: teach the model your data by fine-tuning, or fetch your data at question time with retrieval. The industry default has quietly become retrieval, but the reasoning behind that default is worth understanding, because sometimes it is wrong. Drawing on shipped projects from publishing and e-commerce, we compare the two approaches on cost, freshness, auditability, failure modes, and maintenance burden. You will get a decision framework you can defend in front of both your CTO and your finance team, plus the war stories that shaped it.

sessions.title: Agents That Edit Your CMS
sessions.description: The next wave of AI integration is not chat — it is delegation. AI agents can now create content types, configure fields, build views, and restructure taxonomies from a plain-language instruction. This session shows agentic CMS editing working live, then pulls the curtain back: how agents decide which tools to call, how permission boundaries keep them from redecorating your production site, how to audit what they did, and what happens when they get it confidently wrong. We close with a frank assessment of which site-building tasks agents genuinely handle well today and which still need a human with opinions.

sessions.title: Evaluating LLM Output at Scale
sessions.description: Shipping an AI feature without evaluation is shipping a bug generator with good manners. This session covers building evaluation harnesses for generative features in production: golden datasets that stay relevant as your content drifts, LLM-as-judge pipelines and their well-documented biases, regression detection when you swap model versions, and the statistics of deciding whether output B is actually better than output A or you are staring at noise. Expect real numbers from real eval suites, including the embarrassing ones. If you have already shipped an LLM feature and lie awake wondering if it got worse last Tuesday, this is for you.

sessions.title: AI on a Budget: Small Models, Big Wins
sessions.description: Not every task needs a frontier model, and your invoice knows it. This talk is a practical tour of doing useful AI work with small, cheap, sometimes self-hosted models: classification, tagging, summarisation, extraction, and moderation. We benchmark small open-weight models against the big names on real CMS workloads, show where the quality cliff actually is, and demonstrate a routing pattern that sends easy requests to cheap models and hard ones upmarket. You will leave with concrete numbers for the cost conversation and a healthy suspicion of the phrase 'just use the best model'.

sessions.title: Hands-on: Build a Site Chatbot
sessions.description: Bring a laptop, leave with a working chatbot grounded in your own content. In this hands-on workshop we set up a complete AI assistant on a CMS: connecting a model provider, indexing content into a vector database, wiring up retrieval so answers cite real pages, and taming the bot with prompts, personas and guardrails. Every step is done together, with instructors circulating — nobody gets left behind at the embeddings stage. Basic site-building experience is enough; no AI experience or maths required. A prepared codebase and cloud credentials are provided, so setup pain is limited to the venue Wi-Fi.

sessions.title: Zero-Downtime Deploys for Mortals
sessions.description: Deploying should be boring, and this talk is about how to make it that way without a platform team or a six-figure tooling budget. We walk through the anatomy of a safe release for a database-backed website: health checks that check actual health, database updates that tolerate two code versions running at once, feature flags as a decoupling tool, and the rollback you rehearse before you need it. Everything is demonstrated on an unremarkable LAMP-ish stack on purpose. If deploys at your organisation involve a maintenance page and a prayer, come along — mortality is the target audience.

sessions.title: Kubernetes Without Tears
sessions.description: Kubernetes has a reputation problem: essential to some, incomprehensible to most, and oversold to nearly everyone. This session is an honest tour for web teams considering the jump. We cover what the cluster genuinely gives you — self-healing, declarative environments, sane scaling — and what it costs in cognitive overhead, YAML archaeology, and 3am novelty. Then we look at the middle paths: managed platforms, lighter orchestrators, and the perfectly respectable option of not using it at all. You will leave with a clear-eyed checklist for whether your team should adopt Kubernetes, wait, or run away smiling.

sessions.title: Observability: Logs, Metrics, Traces
sessions.description: When production misbehaves, the difference between a five-minute fix and a five-hour incident is usually whether you can see what is happening. This talk untangles the three pillars of observability and what each is actually for: logs tell you what happened, metrics tell you how much and how often, traces tell you where the time went. We instrument a realistic web application live using open standards, then use that telemetry to hunt down a slow checkout flow. Practical, vendor-neutral, and focused on the questions you will ask at 3am rather than the dashboards that look good in vendor demos.

sessions.title: Postmortems That Don't Assign Blame
sessions.description: Something broke, customers noticed, and now everyone is in a meeting looking at their shoes. What happens next determines whether your team learns or just gets better at hiding mistakes. This session covers running blameless postmortems that people actually want to attend: reconstructing timelines without interrogation, asking 'how did the system make this easy to get wrong' instead of 'who did it', writing action items that get done, and handling the manager who really, really wants a name. Includes a lightly fictionalised walkthrough of a real incident review, played for both laughs and lessons.

sessions.title: Chaos Engineering on a Friday
sessions.description: You do not know how your system fails until it fails, and production is a terrible place to find out by surprise. This session covers deliberately injecting failure — killed processes, saturated disks, severed network links, misbehaving dependencies — in a controlled, hypothesis-driven way. We share the game-day format we run with client teams, the blast-radius controls that keep experiments from becoming incidents, and the organisational groundwork required before you unplug anything on purpose. Expect real experiment results, including the ones that ruined a perfectly good Friday. Solid production operations experience assumed; this is not an introduction to monitoring.

sessions.title: GitOps: Your Cluster as a Pull Request
sessions.description: If your infrastructure changes happen in a terminal and live only in the memory of whoever typed them, you have configuration drift with extra steps. GitOps flips the model: the repository is the source of truth, and an operator continuously reconciles reality against it. Every change is a pull request — reviewed, auditable, revertable. This talk demonstrates the full loop on a live cluster: merge, reconcile, verify, and then the fun part, watching the system heal itself when we sabotage it by hand. We also cover the awkward corners: secrets, emergency changes, and what 'revert' means when a migration already ran.

sessions.title: The Art of the Rollback
sessions.description: Everyone has a deployment strategy; fewer have a retreat strategy. This lightning talk makes the case that the rollback is a first-class feature of your delivery pipeline, not an emergency improvisation. In fifteen brisk minutes: why roll-forward culture quietly increases risk, the database migration patterns that keep your escape hatch open, why you should rehearse rollbacks like fire drills, and the one-line change that made our team stop fearing releases. You will leave with a checklist short enough to actually adopt and a new respect for the undo button.

sessions.title: Scaling Databases Past the Comfort Zone
sessions.description: There is a moment in every growing system's life when the database stops being furniture and starts being a character in the story. This session is about what comes after: read replicas and their consistency traps, connection pooling at the point where it becomes load-bearing, partitioning strategies and the queries they quietly break, online schema changes on tables measured in billions of rows, and the long, political road to sharding. Every pattern comes with an incident timeline from production. Bring your hardest database problem; the Q&A doubles as free consulting.

sessions.title: CSS Has Grown Up: Grid, Container Queries, :has()
sessions.description: If you learned CSS in the float era and have been apologising to it ever since, this session is your amnesty. Modern CSS quietly solved most of the problems we built JavaScript mountains to work around: container queries make components genuinely responsive to their context, :has() gives us the parent selector we swore would never exist, subgrid fixes alignment across nesting, and native layers end the specificity wars. Everything is live-coded against a real component library, no slides pretending to be code. You will leave wanting to delete JavaScript, which is the correct emotional response.

sessions.title: Accessibility Is Not a Checklist
sessions.description: Teams that treat accessibility as a compliance checkbox produce websites that technically pass and practically fail. This session resets the frame. Through live demonstrations with a screen reader and keyboard-only navigation, you will experience your typical page the way many of your users do — and feel exactly where it falls apart. We then work backwards to the habits that prevent those failures: semantic HTML as the default, focus management as a design concern, and testing with assistive tech as routine rather than ritual. No prior accessibility knowledge needed. Discomfort guaranteed, in the useful way.

sessions.title: Design Tokens for Multi-brand Sites
sessions.description: One design system, five brands, three themes, and a marketing department with opinions: welcome to multi-brand front-end architecture. Design tokens are how you survive it. This talk covers structuring token hierarchies that map to brand decisions rather than CSS properties, the naming debates worth having and the ones to skip, pipelines that turn a designer's Figma change into a reviewed pull request, and dark mode as a first-class theme rather than an afterthought filter. Based on three years and two painful rewrites of a production token system serving very different brands from one codebase.

sessions.title: Web Components in the Real World
sessions.description: Web components promised framework-free reuse; production had follow-up questions. After shipping a component library used across dozens of properties and several frameworks, we have answers with scars attached. This session gets specific: shadow DOM styling boundaries and the theming API you must design deliberately, server-side rendering with declarative shadow DOM, form participation via ElementInternals, event contracts that survive framework wrappers, and the accessibility gotchas nobody blogs about. If you are evaluating web components for a serious design system — or maintaining one and collecting grievances — this is your support group with slides.

sessions.title: Stop Shipping JavaScript
sessions.description: A polemic in fifteen minutes: most of the JavaScript on your site is solving problems the platform already solved. Accordions, dialogs, form validation, lazy loading, smooth scrolling, view transitions — we replace each one live with the native HTML and CSS equivalent, and watch the bundle shrink and the Lighthouse score climb. This is not a framework-hate talk; it is a triage discipline. The JavaScript you keep matters more when it is the JavaScript only JavaScript can do. Warning: contains deletion of beloved dependencies and at least one standing ovation for the details element.

sessions.title: Dark Mode Done Right
sessions.description: Dark mode is easy to add and easy to add badly, as anyone who has seen pure-black backgrounds with vibrating saturated text can attest. In fifteen minutes: the prefers-color-scheme media query and the three-state toggle users actually expect, the color-scheme property that fixes your form controls for free, why you desaturate brand colours and lift shadows instead of inverting them, elevation in a world without believable drop shadows, and handling the user's choice without a flash of the wrong theme. Small talk, immediately applicable, your users' retinas will thank you.

sessions.title: Single Directory Components Deep Dive
sessions.description: Single directory components changed how Drupal themes are built: template, styles, script, and schema co-located, discoverable, and composable. This deep dive goes well past the getting-started guide. We cover component schemas as contracts and what validation buys you in practice, slots versus props and the API design instincts behind the choice, composing components across themes and modules without inheritance spaghetti, integration with render arrays and the page-builder layer, and performance characteristics under real load. Comfortable Twig and theme-layer knowledge is assumed. Bring your architectural grievances; we have opinions and benchmarks.

sessions.title: Prototyping with Real Content
sessions.description: Lorem ipsum is a lie your design tells itself. Real headlines are too long, real names have apostrophes and diacritics, real product descriptions are three words or three paragraphs, and real photos are the wrong aspect ratio. This session makes the case for prototyping with production-shaped content from the first sketch, and shows how: quick content audits, structured sample data that mirrors your actual field lengths and edge cases, and the workshop trick of making stakeholders write the placeholder text themselves. Your layouts will get uglier immediately and your shipped product will get dramatically better. Fair trade.

sessions.title: API-First CMS: Decouple Without Regret
sessions.description: Decoupling your CMS from your front end promises freedom and delivers, on average, three new problems per freedom. This session is a field guide from someone who has shipped both triumphant and regrettable decoupled builds. We cover the real decision criteria — editorial preview, personalisation, performance, team shape — then the architecture that keeps you sane: API design that survives redesigns, preview systems editors trust, caching layers that respect content freshness, and the escape hatches for the pages that never needed decoupling. Includes the checklist we now run before any client hears the word 'headless'.

sessions.title: Queues Save Lives
sessions.description: The user clicks 'save', and your code decides to resize four images, notify three services, rebuild a search index, and send an email — all before responding. This talk is about the moment you learn to say 'later'. We introduce queue-based thinking from first principles: which work belongs in the request and which does not, at-least-once delivery and why your jobs must tolerate déjà vu, retries with backoff, dead-letter queues as inbox for your mistakes, and monitoring depth before your users monitor it for you. Concepts throughout are demonstrated with a plain database-backed queue — no exotic infrastructure required to start.

sessions.title: What's New in Modern PHP
sessions.description: If your PHP habits fossilised around 7.4, the language has been quietly getting better without you. This tour covers what changed and why it matters for real codebases: property hooks that finally end getter-setter boilerplate, asymmetric visibility, enums as domain modelling workhorses, readonly classes and the immutability idiom, first-class callables, fibers and where async PHP genuinely helps, and the JIT's actual (modest) impact on web workloads. Each feature comes with a before-and-after from production code, not a synthetic snippet. Guaranteed at least one 'wait, PHP does that now?' moment or your attendance fee back.

sessions.title: Designing Webhooks People Can Trust
sessions.description: Webhooks are the API surface everyone ships and nobody designs. Then the retries duplicate a payment, a consumer goes down for an hour, and suddenly everyone cares. This session collects the hard-won patterns of reliable event delivery: signing and verification done properly, idempotency keys and consumer-side dedup, ordered delivery and why you should stop promising it, retry policies with jitter and give-up strategies, replay tooling for when consumers need history, and the observability that turns 'did you get our webhook?' from an email thread into a query. For engineers who operate webhook systems in anger — payload design bikeshedding not included.

sessions.title: The Repository Pattern Is Overrated
sessions.description: A fifteen-minute heresy, delivered with love. The repository pattern — as commonly implemented in web codebases — is an abstraction tax paid for a portability that never arrives. We look at real examples where the repository layer added indirection without insulation, mocked itself into meaningless tests, and duplicated the ORM's API one method at a time. Then the constructive turn: what to do instead, when a query is just a query, and the narrower cases where the pattern genuinely earns its keep. Come to cheer, come to heckle; the speaker has survived worse code review threads than your Q&A.

sessions.title: Search: From LIKE to Vectors
sessions.description: Every site search starts as a LIKE query and somebody's optimism, and every growing site eventually outgrows both. This session walks the full evolutionary ladder with live demos at each rung: naive SQL matching and where it collapses, inverted indexes and the relevance scoring that powers classic full-text search, faceting and typo tolerance, and then the new rung — semantic search with embeddings, where 'talks about deploying safely' finds the CI/CD session without sharing a single keyword. We finish with hybrid retrieval, combining keyword precision with semantic recall. One codebase, one dataset, five search engines, honest trade-offs throughout.

sessions.title: Caching Layers Explained with Sandwiches
sessions.description: Caching is the art of not doing work twice, and websites do it in more places than most developers realise. Using an extended and increasingly strained sandwich-shop metaphor, we tour every layer: the browser cache, the CDN at the edge, the reverse proxy, the application's render cache, and the database's own buffers. For each layer you will learn what it stores, when it lies to you, and how invalidation actually works — including cache tags, the genuinely elegant answer to 'the homepage is still showing the old headline'. By the end, 'why am I seeing stale content' becomes a question you can answer methodically. Sandwiches not provided.

sessions.title: Event Sourcing: A Cautionary Tale
sessions.description: We event-sourced our order management system, and this talk is the postmortem nobody made us write. It began beautifully: a perfect audit log, time travel through state, elegant projections. Then came schema evolution against an immutable history, replays measured in hours, eventual consistency confusing every support conversation, and GDPR deletion requests colliding with an append-only store. This is a detailed, technical account of what worked, what buckled, and the hybrid architecture we landed on — event sourcing where the audit trail pays for the complexity, boring CRUD everywhere else. Come for the schadenfreude, stay for the decision framework.

sessions.title: Your First Core Contribution
sessions.description: The gap between 'I use this software' and 'I contribute to this software' looks like a canyon and is actually a kerb. This session walks you across it, live: we find a real novice-friendly issue in the queue, set up the development environment, reproduce the bug, write the fix, add the test, and submit the merge request — with all the hesitations and small stumbles left in, because that is what real contribution looks like. Along the way you will learn how issues are triaged, what reviewers actually look for, and the etiquette that makes maintainers happy to see your name. You need basic PHP or JavaScript; courage is provided.

sessions.title: Maintaining a Module Without Burning Out
sessions.description: Congratulations, your side project has users now — which means it has bug reports, feature demands, security advisories, and a stranger who is disappointed in you. This session is a sustainability manual for open source maintainers: triage systems that keep the queue honest without consuming your evenings, saying no with kindness and a template, recruiting co-maintainers before you desperately need them, security releases without panic, and the deprecation policies that let you delete code guilt-free. Also, seriously considered: when to hand a project over, and when to let it die with dignity. Your future self sends thanks.

sessions.title: Running a Local Meetup That Survives
sessions.description: Tech meetups have a lifecycle: enthusiastic launch, strong first year, slow fade, apologetic final post. Seven years in, ours is still growing, and this talk is the operations manual. The unglamorous machinery that matters: venue arrangements that survive sponsor churn, a speaker pipeline that does not depend on the organiser's friends, the two-organiser rule that prevents single-point-of-burnout, formats for the months when no one submits a talk, and how to measure health by return rate rather than headcount. Whether you are starting a meetup or resuscitating one, you will leave with a checklist and renewed nerve.

sessions.title: Funding Open Source: What Actually Works
sessions.description: The software your business depends on is maintained by volunteers, and the volunteers are tired. As an engineering leader I have tried most of the ways money meets open source: sponsorships, bounties, support contracts, hiring maintainers outright, and donating staff time on company hours. This talk is the honest accounting — what each approach cost, what it produced, which ones distorted the projects they meant to help, and the depressingly simple option that outperformed the clever ones. With budget numbers real enough to take to your own leadership, and a hard look at why 'exposure' still is not a currency.

sessions.title: Mentoring at Scale
sessions.description: One mentor and one mentee is a friendship; a mentorship programme is infrastructure. After onboarding over a thousand first-time contributors between us, we can tell you which infrastructure holds. This session covers designing contribution events where newcomers do real work rather than tutorial theatre, training experienced developers to mentor without taking the keyboard, matching strategies, the follow-up cadence that turns weekend enthusiasm into a second and third contribution, and measuring retention honestly. Includes our full sprint-day playbook, printable and shamelessly stealable. For community organisers, team leads, and anyone who remembers being lost and wants to pay that debt forward.

sessions.title: Docs Are a Love Language
sessions.description: Nobody reads the docs — until 2am, mid-incident, when the docs become the most important text in their life. This lightning talk is a love letter to documentation and a fifteen-minute masterclass in writing it: the four kinds of docs and why mixing them produces mush, writing for the reader who is skimming in a panic, examples as the primary interface, the empty-states and error messages that are documentation in disguise, and the review question that improves any page — 'what does the reader want to do right now?'. Bring the URL of your project's worst page; you will know exactly how to fix it by the end.

sessions.title: From Issue Queue to Release Manager
sessions.description: There is a career ladder inside open source that nobody posts job listings for: triager, reviewer, committer, release manager. This session maps it from the inside. We get specific about the invisible senior work — reading a hundred issues to understand which three matter, reviews that teach rather than gatekeep, release engineering under semantic versioning promises, coordinating security embargoes across volunteers in nine time zones, and the trust dynamics of getting and granting commit access. For experienced contributors ready to take on stewardship, and for employers who want to understand what their staff engineers are actually doing in the issue queue all day.

sessions.title: Contribution Sprint Bootcamp
sessions.description: Spend the final day of camp becoming an open source contributor — for real, with your name on a merge request by sundown. This full-day, hands-on bootcamp takes you through the entire lifecycle: development environment setup that actually works on the first try, navigating the issue queue to find a task matched to your skills, reproducing and fixing a real issue, writing the test, submitting the change, and responding to your first code review. Mentors work with you in small pods, and documentation, design, and testing contributions count just as much as code. All skill levels welcome; the only prerequisite is a laptop and mild stubbornness.
